summary: In this interview, Ian Goodfellow discusses the limits and definitions of deep learning, viewing neural networks as multi-step programs rather than mere hierarchical representations. He explains his evolving views on adversarial examples, the origins and mechanics of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and his approach to writing about rapidly changing AI fields.

principles.statement: Deep learning is a component of a bigger system, not the entire ingredient of intelligence.
principles.rationale: Current AI systems use deep learning as sub-modules (like estimating value functions in AlphaGo) rather than relying on it as a standalone solution for general intelligence.
principles.source_quote: So far, nobody is really proposing to have only what you'd call deep learning as the entire ingredient of intelligence. You use deep learning as sub-modules of other systems.

principles.statement: Deep learning is defined by learning parameters over multiple consecutive steps, not just by gradient descent.
principles.rationale: Defining deep learning strictly by gradient descent excludes valid deep models like Boltzmann machines or networks trained via genetic algorithms. The defining characteristic is the sequence of parameterized operations.
principles.source_quote: I would say deep learning is any kind of machine learning that involves learning parameters of more than one consecutive step.

principles.statement: Adversarial examples are primarily a security liability rather than a fundamental flaw in machine learning compared to human cognition.
principles.rationale: While initially viewed as a gap between human and machine perception, adversarial examples are better understood practically as vulnerabilities that malicious actors can exploit in deployed systems.
principles.source_quote: I think of them now more of as a security liability than as an issue that necessarily shows there something uniquely wrong with machine learning as opposed to humans.

principles.statement: Scaling up algorithms with multimodal data is a promising path toward human-level cognition.
principles.rationale: Humans learn from integrated senses and diverse experiences. Exposing machine learning models to large amounts of multimodal data that allows them to close the loop and interact will likely yield significant breakthroughs.
principles.source_quote: I think when you get that kind of integrated dataset working with a machine learning model that can actually close the loop and interact, we may find that algorithms not so different from what we have today; learn really interesting things when you scale them up a lot and a large amount of multimodal data.

frameworks.name: Writing for Rapidly Evolving Fields
frameworks.when_to_apply: When summarizing a fast-moving technical field for a textbook or reference guide.
steps: Wait and observe the field to see which concepts stand the test of time.
Focus on providing a high-level summary that gives readers the language and core concepts needed to understand the field.
Acknowledge rapidly evolving areas without detailing the exact current best architectures, as specific performance metrics and models will quickly become outdated.
frameworks.source_quote: You should know that learning-to-learn is a thing and that it may very well be the source of the latest and greatest convolutional net... But there isn't a lot of point in trying to summarize exactly which architecture in which learning approach got to which level of performance.

mental_models.name: Neural Networks as Multi-step Programs
mental_models.description: Instead of viewing layers as learning specific hierarchical representations (e.g., edges to corners to grandmother cells), view them as a program that refines a thought or state over multiple sequential updates.
mental_models.example: In a ResNet, the network takes one particular kind of representation and updates it several times, refining it carefully until it's good enough to use, which is akin to a form of reasoning.

mental_models.name: The Machine Learning Triad
mental_models.description: Decomposing machine learning algorithms into three distinct pieces: the model (the structure/function that makes predictions), the optimization algorithm (how parameters are updated), and the dataset (how the world is represented).
mental_models.example: Deep learning defines the model (having multiple layers), while gradient descent is just one possible optimization algorithm used to update it.

mental_models.name: GANs as a Two-Player Game
mental_models.description: Modeling generative generation as a game theoretic competition between a Generator (creating fake data from scratch) and a Discriminator (guessing real vs fake), leading to a Nash equilibrium.
mental_models.example: Training a model to generate cat photos by having the generator try to fool the discriminator, until the generator captures the correct probability distribution and the discriminator is reduced to random guessing.

heuristics: To make a system robust, do a worst-case analysis and ensure it works against adversarial examples.
When writing about a fast-moving field, teach the vocabulary and core concepts rather than the latest state-of-the-art benchmarks.
Lowered inhibitions can help you try out ambitious ideas you would normally shoot down.

signature_quotes.text: I already kind of think of neural nets as a kind of program. I think of deep learning as basically learning programs that have more than one step.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing whether neural networks could be made to reason like symbolic systems.

signature_quotes.text: I would say deep learning is any kind of machine learning that involves learning parameters of more than one consecutive step.
I think of them now more of as a security liability than as an issue that necessarily shows there something uniquely wrong with machine learning as opposed to humans.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his evolved view on adversarial examples.

signature_quotes.text: A lot of the time with machine learning algorithms it's really hard to predict ahead of time how well they'll actually perform. You have to just run the experiment and see what happens.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why GANs worked for photo generation when deep Boltzmann machines struggled.

signature_quotes.text: When your inhibitions are lowered you're more willing to try out things that you wouldn't try out otherwise.
signature_quotes.context: Joking about coming up with the idea for GANs at a bar.

anti_patterns.description: Defining deep learning strictly as gradient descent applied to differentiable functions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It excludes valid deep models like Boltzmann machines or models trained with evolutionary algorithms that still involve multiple parameterized steps.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming adversarial training always improves clean accuracy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: While it worked for MNIST against weak adversaries, on other datasets and against stronger adversaries, there is a trade-off where you have to give up clean accuracy to gain robustness.

anti_patterns.description: Trying to write a reference book that covers the absolute latest state-of-the-art in a fast-moving field.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The specific architectures and performance levels become outdated quickly; it is pointless to summarize exactly which architecture got which level of performance.
